items.q: Did the Treaty of Versailles directly cause WWII?
items.correct: It contributed via German resentment and economic strain, but the 1930s Depression, Hitler's choices, and Allied appeasement were equally necessary — Versailles alone is not sufficient.
items.misconception: Versailles caused WWII directly because it was so harsh on Germany.
items.random_wrong: Versailles caused WWII because it required Germany to invade Poland.
items.why: Comparable peace terms have not always led to war. Modern historians stress contingency; treating Versailles as sufficient cause is a simple but inadequate story.

items.q: What were the Wars of the Roses really about?
items.correct: Dynastic conflict between rival branches of the Plantagenets (Lancaster and York) over the throne, intensified by weak kingship under Henry VI and noble factionalism.
items.misconception: The whole country was at war for thirty continuous years.
items.random_wrong: They were a religious war between Catholics and Protestants.
items.why: Fighting was sporadic and most of England saw no battles; ordinary life carried on. Students imagine total civil war; in reality, it was an aristocratic dispute fought in short campaigns.

items.q: Why did the Schlieffen Plan fail in 1914?
items.correct: Belgian resistance delayed the German advance; Russia mobilised faster than expected; Moltke weakened the right wing; and the French rallied at the Marne.
items.misconception: The Schlieffen Plan failed because Britain entered the war.
items.random_wrong: The Schlieffen Plan failed because it tried to invade Russia first.
items.why: Britain's army was small in 1914; failure was about timing and logistics, not British intervention. Students overestimate the BEF's role in the initial defeat.

items.q: On what date was the Armistice signed?
items.why: Correct answer: 11 November 1918. “28 June 1919” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which article of Versailles imposed war guilt on Germany?
items.why: Correct answer: Article 231. “Article 14” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many British casualties were suffered on the first day of the Somme (1 July 1916)?
items.why: The real figure is 57470. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
